title: Labor cheat sheet on how to address broken promises leaked
text:
H﻿ello and welcome to our live rolling coverage of the 2026 Federal Budget, due to be handed down by Treasurer Jim Chalmers tonight.

It is widely expected that the budget will involve changes to negative gearing and the capital gains tax, two changes that could change Australia's property and taxation system for generations.

There is also speculation that a one-off tax time payment of up to $300 is coming for most income earners in the country.

READ MORE: In just a few hours, Aussie property will change forever

READ MORE: What do everyday Aussies actually want from tonight's budget?

READ MORE: ﻿PM defends sweeping tax reforms that could hit property investors hard

title: In just a few hours, Aussie property will change forever
text:
A﻿NALYSIS: Today's federal budget could be the most consequential in decades as the government stands poised to unveil a major tax reform package and unforgiving spending cuts.

In just hours, Treasurer Jim Chalmers will hand down ﻿"ambitious" and "responsible" budget papers that outline his plan to address intergenerational inequity, and help fight rising inflation and government debt.﻿

He has tried to downplay the tax reform package, telling reporters last week that the main game is creating billions in savings.

SCROLL DOWN FOR DETAILS ON WHAT AUSTRALIANS CAN EXPECT IN THE BUDGET﻿

READ MORE: ﻿ PM defends sweeping tax reforms that could hit property investors hard

BUDGET LIVE UPDATES: Negative gearing changes expected within hours

Finance Minister Katy Gallagher, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and Treasurer Dr Jim Chalmers are preparing to drop a monumental shift on the Australian economy. (Alex Ellinghausen)

"What you will see in the budget is, in gross terms, there will be more dollars in savings than dollars in revenue upgrades. There will also be more dollars in savings than dollars in tax reform," he said last week.

"The point that I'm making there is that savings and spending restraint is doing a lot of the heavy lifting in the very responsible budget that (Finance Minister Katy Gallagher) and I will hand down."

But the all-but-confirmed changes to the capital gains tax (CGT) discount and negative gearing are what many have their eyes on.

Australia's property industry has been the backbone of the economy - and it's about to undergo a massive shift. (LUIS ENRIQUE ASCUI)

KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne said this was the most "consequential" budget for taxpayers in about a decade.

"In many regards, assuming that everything that we've already been told is true, there's a lot more in this budget than what we've seen for quite some time," he said.

"Particularly around the taxation side of the budget, some of the changes are quite meaningful from a policy perspective.

"I suppose what's also going to be interesting as well is from an expenditure side is what the government is proposing in terms of hopefully pulling back nominal expenditure so actually a decline in government spending as opposed to just flatlining that expenditure or even having a small increase."

As far as Rynne can remember, this could be the most meaningful budget since Joe Hockey's first in 2014.

Momentum has been building for the government to tackle ﻿controversial tax breaks for property investors. (Dion Georgopoulos)

Momentum has been building for the government to tackle the ﻿generous twin tax breaks of negative gearing and the CGT discount after a Greens-led Senate inquiry in March supported what some critics have said - the two concessions ﻿are flawed and benefit investors over first-home buyers.

And while the government has not confirmed these changes will be in the budget, they have not ruled them out either, despite being given plenty of opportunities to do so.

Supporters of the policies, like the Housing Industry Association, believe the changes could worsen the rental crisis by discouraging property investment during a housing crisis.

However, the government is framing its budget policies in social terms, arguing the changes are necessary to address intergenerational inequity. ﻿

"We've... got serious challenges in the housing market when it comes to how difficult it is for people, particularly young people, to get a toehold in the market," Chalmers said on Sunday. ﻿

Here is everything we expect to see change when the budget is handed down today:

$300 more back at tax time

One of the speculated moves is the federal government offering taxpayers an earned income offset, which could see people paying between $200 and $300 less at tax time this year.

﻿The report in The Australian framed the offset as a one-off ﻿cost-of-living relief measure, rather than an ongoing offer.

Albanese has refused to confirm whether the offset will be included in the budget.

﻿"It's a whole lot of speculation out there in budgets and that's what happens. Some of it right, some of it's wrong," he said last week.

Big savings ﻿

In a press conference with Finance Minister Katy Gallagher, Chalmers said the budget would be ﻿more concentrated on responsible spending and savings than the anticipated tax reforms.

"What you will see in the budget is, in gross terms, there will be more dollars in savings than dollars in revenue upgrades. There will also be more dollars in savings than dollars in tax reform," he said last week.

"The point that I'm making there is that savings and spending restraint is doing a lot of the heavy lifting in the very responsible budget that Katy and I will hand down."

Gallagher said the budget will include savings from every government department, with savings already identified in defence, NDIS and external labour.

"This is essentially extending the existing savings measure we have put in place in previous budgets, and it will also have significant savings from unallocated funding across a number of departments," she said.

The Australian Financial Review reported the public service is facing sweeping job cuts, with thousands of positions to be lost under the budget's spending cuts.﻿

Australia's tax system is set for an overhaul. (Getty)

EV tax breaks wound back ﻿

Chalmers and Energy Minister Chris Bowen last week announced they will wind back the electric vehicle fringe benefits tax (FBT) discount from April 2027 in a move estimated to create $1.7 billion in savings over five years. ﻿

"The current New Vehicle Efficiency Standards has seen a dramatic increase in the availability of affordable EV models, and now is the right time to focus the FBT exemption on these cars," they said in a joint statement.

"The new rules will encourage manufacturers to offer more affordable and cheaper to run EVs in the Australian market."

From April 2027, the full discount will only apply to electric vehicles costing $75,000 or less.

Electric vehicles costing more than $75,000 ﻿but less than the luxury car tax threshold will receive a 25 per cent discount on their tax.

From April 2029, ﻿all electric vehicles below the luxury car threshold will receive the 25 per cent discount.

The changes come after the popular ﻿policy caused a surge in electric vehicles on Australian roads and cost the budget $1.4 billion in 2025-26, up from the initial $90 million forecast.

NDIS cuts ﻿

Health Minister Mark Butler announced $15 billion would be cut from the ﻿National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) over the next four years to clamp down on ballooning costs and prevent it from becoming "an ATM for shonks, grifters, fraudsters and crooks".

Changes include tighter criteria, standardised and evidence-based assessments, fraud prevention and reduced spending on social and community participation per participant and daily activities.

Initial modelling shows the changes will reduce the number of people on the NDIS from 760,000 to 600,000 by the end of the decade. ﻿

"It costs too much and is growing too fast," Butler said in April.

"We can't afford for the NDIS to continue growing at its current rate."﻿

The government is cutting billions from the NDIS. (Getty)

Defence boost

Defence will receive an additional $53 billion over the next decade, increasing spending to 3 per cent of Australia's GDP by 2033 under the NATO methodology.

US President Donald Trump has been pressuring Australia and NATO countries to raise their defence spending, complaining that the US does a lot of the heavy lifting.

The funding boost, announced last month by Defence Minister Richard Marles, was partly paid for by the sale of military land and will deliver greater warfare capabilities, systems and communications.

Rail line between Sydney and Canberra

An extra $100 million will be provided for ﻿upgrades to the rail line between Sydney and Canberra.

The federal government will foot half the bill, while the NSW and ACT governments contribute $25 million each. ﻿

There will be about five years of ongoing upgrades, including investigations into new express services, new boom gates and lights at level crossings, improvements to track alignments and turnouts and station and stabling improvements. ﻿

The 321-kilometre corridor includes the Sydney Trains-operated line between Sydney Central station and Macarthur, the Southern Line through the Southern Highlands to Goulburn, and the Country Regional Network branch line into Canberra.

The government has previously set aside almost $2.8 billion for the rail network and building a high-speed rail between Newcastle and Sydney.

Petrol has become a major factor in tonight's budget. (Steven Siewert)

Cheaper fuel

The federal government halved the fuel excise and removed the Heavy Vehicle Road User Charge ﻿from April 1 to June 30 to provide some relief for motorists facing soaring fuel costs, which will be funded in the budget.

The measure was estimated to reduce fuel costs by ﻿26.3 cents per litre and almost $19 on a 65-litre tank.

The Australian ﻿Competition and Consumer Commission is monitoring fuel prices across the country to ensure the cost savings are being passed on.

Chalmers has ruled out extending the fuel excise cut and the removal of the Heavy Vehicle Road User Charge after June.

More announcements ﻿

$10 billion fuel security package ﻿to boost the national stockpiles by more than 10 days, extra storage facilities and a study into expanding the country's fuel refining capabilities.

$1.8 billion over five years to make Medicare Urgent Care Clinics permanent.

An additional $25 billion for public hospitals﻿ in the new five-year National Health Reform Agreement.

$74 million over two years to set up a national centre to fight terrorism and online threats. ﻿

$500 million to speed up housing approvals through environmental law reforms.

$1000 instant receipt-free tax deduction ﻿for work-related expenses for about 6.2 million workers.

Reducing the private health insurance rebate for those over 65 to the same level as everyone else.

﻿New Payday Super rules mandating employers to pay superannuation at the same time as salary and wage payments will come into effect from July 1.

title: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer fighting to keep job
text:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer is defying growing calls for him to quit despite more than 70 UK Labour MPs publicly calling for him to stand down.

In a speech overnight, he pledged to "prove his doubters wrong" and to move the UK closer to the European Union in a bid to shore up his leadership.

It came after the ruling Labour Party suffered major losses last week in local elections across England and legislative votes in Scotland and Wales.

BUDGET LIVE UPDATES: Negative gearing changes expected within hours

British PM Keir Starmer attempted to revive his leadership in a speech to supporters in London. (Getty)

"I know I have my doubters and I know I need to prove them wrong, and I will," Starmer said during a speech in London intended to kickstart his fightback. ﻿

But it appeared to have little impact, with at least 71 Labour MPs, from the 403 in Parliament, calling for him to release a timetable for his resignation.

The BBC is reporting﻿ Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood is among cabinet ministers urging Starmer to quit, but the cabinet remains split and the majority are backing him to stay.

None of the high-profile Labour politicians considered potential challengers to Starmer, including former Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner, Health Secretary Wes Streeting and Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, has yet called for him to resign.

Over last weekend, Australian-born Labour backbench MP Catherine West threatened to trigger a leadership challenge against the prime minister, but later backed down. She has called for him to leave by September.﻿

READ MORE: Accused Donald Trump assassin pleads not guilty to all charges

UK Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood. (Getty)

Since Starmer's government swept to a landslide election victory in July 2024, it has struggled to deliver promised economic growth, repair tattered public services and ease the cost of living.

It has also had repeated policy backflips on issues such as welfare reforms.﻿

Starmer has been further hurt by his disastrous decision to appoint Peter Mandelson, a scandal-tarnished friend of Jeffrey Epstein, as Britain's ambassador to Washington.

Voters took their frustration out on the government last Thursday when Labour suffered a net loss of more than 1100 local council seats across England, and lost control of several local authorities.

Anti-immigration party Reform UK gained more than 1300 seats across England and made significant gains in legislative elections in Wales and Scotland.﻿

- With Associated Press﻿

title: What do everyday Aussies actually want from tonight's budget?
text:
If public sentiment is anything to go by, everyday Australians are sick of struggling.

As Treasurer Jim Chalmers prepares to hand down his fifth budget, housing affordability, tax reform and rising day-to-day expenses are expected to sit front and centre in Canberra.

Nine.com.au asked Australians what they actually want from tonight's announcement, with many feeling the cost-of-living crisis has no end in sight.

Watch the video above.﻿

Sydney locals Nicholas, Agharia and Brittany share what they want from the budget. (Supplied)

Housing affordability is tipped to be a major focus, with speculation mounting around possible changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax (CGT) discounts.

Brittany, 30, from Manly Vale, had property tax reform at the top of her budget wishlist.

"I'd like to see some new introductions for changes to negative gearing for those who own more than one property," she said.

She argued that investors should be able to negatively gear only one property, and also backed keeping the fuel excise in place despite its impact on inflation.

Housing affordability is tipped to be a major focus tonight. (Supplied)

Bismarck, a property investor from Botany, said investors were closely watching any potential CGT changes as high interest rates continue to bite.

As for young Sydneysider Nicholas, he said Australians needed better education on tax and finances – particularly in understanding how large companies operate.

Others pointed overseas for ideas.

An Australian man who now resides in Singapore said the government should adopt lower tax settings similar to those abroad.

Healthcare and frontline services were also front of mind. (Dylan Coker)

"The tax rate there is 23 per cent ... so if we could do something like Singapore, that would be fantastic," he said.

Infrastructure was another recurring concern raised by younger voters.

BUDGET LIVE UPDATES: Negative gearing changes expected within hours

Agharia, 24, from Sydney, said governments needed to stop approving huge housing developments without the roads, parks and community facilities needed to support them.

Healthcare and frontline services also remained front of mind. Suki, 23, from Annandale, said she wanted to see additional funding directed towards emergency responders, healthcare workers and women's health services.

Electric vehicles featured in conversations around cost-of-living relief as well.

One woman said she and her husband were preparing to buy an EV and hoped the budget would include incentives to lower prices, as well as possible tax relief for investment properties.

The comments come as the government prepares to wind back some EV tax concessions from 2027. The full fringe benefits tax exemption is set to apply only to vehicles costing less than $75,000.

READ MORE: Australia is just hours away from seeing what could be the most consequential budget in decades

Everyday Aussies have delivered a brutal verdict ahead of tomorrow's budget. (Supplied)

Another man said he wanted politicians to seriously examine proposals to tax the extraction of Australia's natural resources more heavily.

"We have to do it... it's just a no-brainer," he said.

Meanwhile, there's already some indication of what could be included, with reports suggesting workers may receive up to $300 back at tax time through an offset – although the government is yet to confirm the measure.

Departmental spending cuts, NDIS reforms, a boost for defence and renewed debate over housing tax breaks are also on the table, with the government flagging billions in savings.

Changes to the NDIS involve stricter eligibility checks and stronger anti-fraud measures aimed at slowing the scheme's ballooning costs.

title: Real estate boss warns of immediate rent hikes if negative gearing changes
text:
A real estate boss has warned landlords may hike rents by up to 30 per cent if proposed changes to negative gearing come into effect.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers is likely to announce sweeping reforms to negative gearing and capital gains tax in tonight's federal budget as the Albanese government looks to tackle issues of housing affordability.

It is not known how much the government will change negative gearing, a generous tax break that allows an investor to deduct expenses related to properties from their taxable income, meaning they pay less at tax time.

LIVE UPDATES: ﻿ Property investors to be hit in budget bombshell

Negative gearing changes could be announced tonight in the budget. (Photo: BeyondImages)

Although the changes could result in lower prices, Nathan Birch from property investment firm Blink Property said renters would feel the pinch from any changes, and warned it could be immediate.

"For Aussie battlers doing it tough, that means a $400 rental will become $550 almost immediately post the budget announcement if changes to negative gearing come into effect," Birch said.

"This will be catastrophic for so many Australians , and the government really needs to consider the wave of homelessness that could follow, particularly for our most vulnerable."

Treasurer Jim Chalmers claims the government now has no choice but to take bold action on housing. (Alex Ellinghausen)

Birch said the changes would have the strongest impact on "mum and dad investors", who were often renters themselves while they leased out their investment property and waited to save.

Birch said this would lead to a knock-on effect that benefited fewer people in the long run.

"When policy changes like this come into play, their own rents go up, leaving them no choice but to raise the rent on their investments," he said.

"It's a very dangerous knock-on effect that I'm not sure the Labor Government has considered adequately – particularly when so many renters and investors are Labor voters."

Treasurer Chalmers said despite promising against it in last year's election, the government had no choice but to take bold action to address the housing crisis.

"﻿There are genuine intergenerational concerns and pressures in our budget, in our tax system, in our housing market and in our economy more broadly," Chalmers said last week.

title: Banks were quick to pass on rate hikes – just not for savers
text:
But the same cannot be said for most customers who hold savings accounts with the banks , with many still waiting to hear if they can expect a higher return on their deposits.

The official cash rate target is now at the same level of 4.35 per cent it was sitting at last February, before the RBA embarked on its brief easing cycle, in a further blow for borrowers already weary from surging inflation.

READ MORE: Evacuated American cruise ship passenger diagnosed with hantavirus

The Commonwealth Bank, ANZ and NAB have not yet revealed whether they will increase the interest rates e (Dominic Lorrimer)

All four major banks will start passing on the higher interest rates to borrowers from this Friday, May 15, in changes announced within hours of the RBA's announcement.

But so far, Westpac is the only one of the Big Four to confirm its changes to savings accounts.

It will pass on the full interest rate hike to its Spend&Save and Westpac Life savings accounts - but only savers who meet the monthly bonus conditions are eligible.

Westpac is also increasing the base rate on its eSaver account by 0.25 per cent*.

The move means Westpac will have the highest ongoing savings rate in the country at 5.75 per cent on its Spend&Save﻿ account, for those who qualify.

﻿AMP has already passed the May interest rate hike onto savers from today, meaning it currently has the highest "no strings attached" savings rate of 5.10 per cent.

This puts it ahead of rival Macquarie Bank, which is increasing its condition-free account to an ongoing rate of 5.00 per cent - but not until next Friday, May 22.

The Commonwealth Bank, NAB and ANZ have all stated that their savings rates are under review.

If previous rate hikes are a guide, the banks will likely follow Westpac in only partially passing on the rate hikes to savers or with strict conditions which force savers to go through hoops to get the full benefit.

Macquarie Bank is increasing the interest rate on its condition-free savings account to 5.00 per cent from May 22. (Dominic Lorrimer)

Following the March rate hike, bonus saver accounts increased by 0.28 percentage points on average but the base rate scarcely moved at all, nudging up just 0.01 per cent, a Canstar analysis found.

Two out of five Australians with a "bonus" savings account fails to jump through the necessary hoops to get the maximum interest rate every month, according to Canstar.

More than one in ten never meet the criteria.﻿

Delaying passing rate hikes on to savers or only partially passing these on with strict conditions helps raise banks' bottom lines.

Last year, the big four banks' profits rose to a collective $43 billion pre-tax , $16.9 billion of which was paid by owner-occupiers with a mortgage.

A new analysis by the Australia Institute has found the banks have joined mining giants Rio Tinto and BHP as the top six most profitable companies in Australia.

* Correction: This article previously stated that Westpac was only increasing its interest rates for savers on its Spend&Save account, which is incorrect.

title: Tough 24 hours ahead for Keir Starmer as doubters call for resignation
text:
British citizens and Labour Party insiders will wake to a fraught and tense UK after Prime Minister Keir Starmer came under renewed pressure to tender his resignation.

Six ministerial aides have stepped down and more than 70 Labour MPs have publicly called for his resignation after last week's local election saw Labour lose 20 per cent of the vote.

Sir Keir is set to meet with his cabinet, which is split on the future of his leadership, early Tuesday morning, UK time.

The embattled prime minister has vowed to prove his "doubters" wrong and pledged his government would be "better" as he tried to convince angry and restless Labour MPs to continue to back him.

"I know that people are frustrated by the state of Britain, frustrated by politics, and some people [are] frustrated with me," Sir Keir said during an address in central London on Monday, local time.

"I know I have my doubters and I know I need to prove them wrong, and I will."

Despite his attempt to reset his leadership, Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood was among a number of cabinet ministers to have urged the PM to set out the timetable to leave.

The calls were originally led by Catherine West, an Australian-born backbencher who has since backed down from her threat of triggering a leadership contest herself, but has again called on the prime minister to leave office by September.

Labour MP Jonathan Hinder told the BBC's Newsnight program that Sir Keir would not be able to hang on to his position.

"The prime minister is going. No prime minister can survive this," he said.

"No doubt about it."

Among the six ministerial aides to step down was Tom Rutland, an aide to the environment minister.

In his resignation letter he stated: "It is clear to me that the prime minister has lost authority not just within the parliamentary Labour Party but across the country and that he will not be able to regain it."

A 'fraught' and 'tense' time ahead

Nick Rowley, a former member of the Downing Street Policy Directorate under prime minister Tony Blair and now an associate professor at ANU's Crawford School of Public Policy, told News Channel trust in Keir Starmer had diminished and his future was "hard to predict".

"There's this growing sense … we have a technocrat, we have someone who said that they were both decent and rigorous. This is a man who used to be the Director of Public Prosecutions in UK — certainly someone who you would want to trust with the detail," he said.

"And yet there's a growing sense within the party and, indeed, within the country: 'What is the point of Keir Starmer?' Because he doesn't seem to be offering that, because of a number of decisions that have been perceived as bad decisions by the public and by the party, particularly his decision to appoint Peter Mandelson as the ambassador to the United States."

Mr Rowley said there was doubt that Sir Keir had the "charisma or ability to actually take the hard decisions as prime minister".

"It's pretty tense … certainly, anybody observing what is going on in the UK at the centre of power would say that Keir Starmer is in a really, really difficult position and may not be prime minister for very much longer."

In total there are 403 Labour MPs in UK parliament.

Some have voiced their support for the prime minister and many have not publicly taken a stance.

Two of Sir Keir's closest allies, Environment Minister Steve Reed and Defence Minister John Healey, entered the prime minister's Downing Street office late on Monday, according to Sky News.

"Changing leader just leads to chaos. We saw what happened under the Tories. Let's learn from their mistakes, not repeat them," Mr Reed said on social media.

How did we get here?

Sir Keir returned Labour to power after 14 years of Conservative rule in 2024 in a landslide election victory.

Loading...

But his popularity among Britons dived as he swerved from one policy misstep to another.

His leadership also became engulfed in a scandal over the appointment and sacking of Peter Mandelson as UK ambassador to Washington, after revelations about the envoy's ties to the sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

The prime minister has failed to spur economic growth as British citizens continue to feel the effects of a years-long cost-of-living pinch, but has been praised for resisting US President Donald Trump over Iran.

Voters issued a damning indictment of Sir Keir's 22 months in power in local elections held on Thursday last week, which saw huge gains for the hard-right Reform UK party and left-wing populists the Greens at Labour's expense.

Labour lost control of the devolved Welsh parliament for the first time and failed to make up ground on the pro-independence Scottish National Party (SNP) in the devolved parliament in Edinburgh.

Nigel Farage's populist Reform UK party won the most seats during last week's local elections.

Sir Keir has signalled that he hopes to stay in power until 2034, but dozens of Labour MPs have made it clear they believe it is time for him to go.

Catherine West, who threatened to trigger a leadership challenge on Monday, said she was collecting the names of Labour MPs who want Sir Keir to set a timetable for the election of a new leader in September.

The Labour leader warned MPs that the party would "never be forgiven" by voters if it imitated the "chaos" of the previous Conservative government, which went through three prime ministers in four months in 2022.

A leadership contest would likely spark a damaging bout of infighting as MPs from the left and right of the party would joust to position their preferred candidate or shore up Sir Keir.

Under party rules, any challenger would need the support of 81 Labour MPs — 20 per cent of the party in parliament — to trigger a contest.

ABC/Wires

title: 'Leaks in the urinals': New exhibition explores media's role in political history
text:
The media usually ranks alongside politicians at the very bottom of surveys on Australia's most trusted occupations and professions.

Nothing new there, in an era when its product is often derided as 'fake news'.

So when some of the nation's current crop of politicians combined with reporters to recognise the parliamentary press gallery's work since Federation, there was never going to be a clamour to get in.

But even so, on the eve of a cyclically important budget for his government, the prime minister led celebrations at the opening of a new exhibition at Old Parliament House that showcases the work of reporters, camera operators, editors and news support staff.

The Press Gallery exhibition at the Museum of Australian Democracy (MoAD) takes visitors inside the original working spaces at Old Parliament House, where reporters worked between 1927 and 1988.

A recreated press gallery office in the new exhibition at the Museum of Australian Democracy. (Museum of Australian Democracy)

For veteran ABC journalist and now-chair of MoAD, Barrie Cassidy, the physical restoration of a slither of the old press gallery workspaces fills a void in the history of Old Parliament House.

"Right up until now this building has captured the role of the politicians, the role of the staff. What was missing was the gallery," he said.

" It was important to give people a sense of how the media worked in this building. "

Barrie Cassidy says the exhibition provides visitors with a sense of how the media worked in the building. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

By his own admission, what's been recreated at MoAD isn't an exact replica of the crammed and chaotic workplace he left behind in the 1980s.

"What happened when the building closed was more than half of it — the gallery — was demolished, so we couldn't recreate it as such," Mr Cassidy said.

But standing in the ABC bureau he unfathomably shared with 17 colleagues, Mr Cassidy remembers clearly the intimate space "not much bigger than a motel room".

"Almost everybody smoked and if you had 17 desks there'd be 17 ash trays. I smoked at the time, and that's probably the reason I gave it up," he laughed.

In the exhibition's faithful recreation, ash trays — these days hygienically empty — remain fairly prominent fixtures on many desks.

The faithful recreations are complete with ashtrays and vintage telephones. (ABC News: Luke Stephenson)

From traditional mastheads to new technologies

The Press Gallery exhibition seeks to capture the evolution of the media that covered political news across the decades, from its arrival in the building when it opened in 1927, through to its move further up Capital Hill in 1988.

When parliament first relocated from Melbourne into its 'temporary' home in Canberra, the gallery was home to roughly 30 media staff — all of them men, and mostly from traditional printed newspaper mastheads.

By the end, after radio and broadcast television newcomers arrived, numbers had expanded to more than 250 men and women, bringing with them new technology to write, record and transmit their work across the country.

A teleprinter operator known as Joan, or "Zazz", in one of the Press Gallery offices in 1973. (National Archives of Australia)

Staff at the museum worked for two years to find the typewriters, teleprinters, microphones, phones, files and notebooks used across this period.

The museum's curator, Amy Lay, led the team whose task it was to find these pieces of history to form the exhibition's displays.

"We have tried really hard to find a lot of the technology," she said.

" Lots of bureaux didn't keep their tech once it got old or obsolete, we've had to go to people's personal collections. "

Amy Lay and her team spent two years sourcing equipment, files and notebooks for the exhibition. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

Indeed, through the generosity and recollections of former news staff or their families, Ms Lay has painstakingly assembled photos, clippings, press identification tags and stories spanning the decades.

"There are tools of the trade like the typewriters, like the tape recorders, but there are also things that are the sum-up of relationships — we have contact books on display that are the embodiment of how a journalist would maintain those relationships in order to do their work," she said.

The Ten Eyewitness News crew outside Parliament House in 1982. Left to right: (back) John Crowther, Peter Heap, Bill Errington, (front) Tim Young, David Bartho. (National Archives of Australia)

And as more modern forms of media entered the gallery, so did the makeup of the workforce and the habits of the news teams jammed into small spaces change.

The arrival of a more permanent crop of female reporters in the late 1960s and 1970s brought some subtle changes to a robust workplace culture.

"Women coming in particular in the 70s, when they did stay around as a group, were pioneers, really, for the women who came after them," Ms Lay observed.

" And some of those who arrived in the 70s stayed for a really long time, and are still part of the press gallery now. "

Some of the equipment on show is from personal collections. (ABC News: Luke Stephenson)

Lifetime achievements

If ever there was a time to restore the press gallery's place in the building that represents its spiritual home, it's probably now.

As an institution, the loose affiliation of media colleagues and competitors is as old as Australia's federal system of government itself, now in its 125th anniversary.

The press gallery benches at Old Parliament House. (Museum of Australian Democracy)

Which is one reason why Anthony Albanese found a few minutes to attend Old Parliament House, a place he'd worked for four years as a junior staffer for his late Labor mentor, Tom Uren, for the exhibition's opening.

The other reason was to honour the lifetime achievements of two stalwart political journalists: Michelle Grattan and Laurie Oakes.

"Both are characterised by three words, all beginning in 'i'," the PM remarked: "intelligence", "integrity" and "independence".

Michelle Grattan has been honoured for her lifetime achievements. (ABC News: Tobias Hunt)

Grattan is the doyenne of the press gallery, having become The Age's first female bureau chief in 1976 and continues prolific filing on national affairs to this day.

Oakes landed spectacular scoops, including the virtually complete 1980 budget before its release, and struck fear into generations of politicians in his multiple roles, mainly with Nine and the now-defunct The Bulletin.

Accepting his award in front of current and former gallery members, a modest Oakes struck a chord with his observation that he felt "like I should be giving someone else a reward for letting me do it".

"I can't think of a better job quite frankly," he said.

Laurie Oakes with former prime minister Bob Hawke. (Supplied)

He defended today's current crop of younger journalists against criticism he regarded as "undeserved".

"Things are just tougher generally for journalists reporting politics and I'm glad I'm not trying to operate in the current environment," Oakes said.

" I reckon under the circumstances, today's gallery does pretty well. "

If walls could talk, those in the bathrooms at Old Parliament House would have a lot to say. (Museum of Australian Democracy)

Like most in the room at Old Parliament House, the legendary newsman still holds fond memories of its tight spaces and close brushes with politicians in unexpected encounters.

"Albo, you mentioned how members of parliament got to know each other in the urinals. Well, journalists, funny enough, got leaks in the urinals as well," he said.

Of course, no names, no sources.

They're secrets the press gallery keeps forever.

title: Live: Carlton boss addresses media after shock Voss resignation
text:
Graham Wright labels disposal efficiency and transition efficiency as two areas Carlton would like to improve in.

He's again asked why it took them nine matches into this year to figure out those shortcomings.

"Overall, when you look at the body of work, 2022 leading into 2023 playing a prelim into 2024 playing finals ... we weren't a long way off," he says.

"We felt we were still really strong in two, three or four areas and needed to improve in other things."

title: My four-year break from booze has led to an unexpected discovery
text:
What started out as a pregnancy four years ago has turned into a game with myself to see how long I can go without alcohol.

The main question people ask me is, "do you feel healthier?"

If I'm honest, not really. I'm no fitter.

There is, however, one major change that I was not expecting — I got to know myself.

Breaking up with the bottle

Giving up alcohol was extremely out of character for me.

I never had an overt problem with alcohol. I did, however, spend my early career as a journalist in Kalgoorlie, where drinking is a social sport, then in Broome, where alcohol is seen as part of the social fabric.

In the gap between having my first and second child, I would reward myself for getting through a tough day of parenting by opening a good bottle of shiraz.

And once it's open, who was I to let that bottle go off? I'd spend the week working my way through it.

But after a forced nine-month break from drinking alcohol, and the realisation that I didn't really miss it, I decided to see how long this feeling would last.

Making the decision to quit

Initially, I was sheepish about going to the bar and refusing a drink. I thought my friends would assume I was pregnant, or trying for a baby.

In the early months of going alcohol-free, people would ask if I was "back on the booze yet". Or they'd push me to have "just one drink".

What have you given up and what did you learn about yourself in the process? We'd love to hear from you.

Eventually they lost interest and gave up, and that's when the penny dropped — they don't really care if I'm drinking or not because they've got the same person with or without the booze.

Shedding my skin, finally

Once that pressure and stigma dissipated, it was as if I had shed a skin to reveal my whole personality: braver and bolder.

I discovered I'm the same person on the dance floor with a soda that I am at midnight, four wines deep.

When it became obvious who my real, no-pressure friends were, I could clearly see who I should spend time with.

My social circle shifted and activities changed. I'd go out to see the people and it became less about the place.

Natalie (right) with her friend and colleague, Erin. (Supplied)

The fun I have on the dance floor with friends late at night is real and lasting and I can remember it the next day without fact-checking in a Sunday debrief session.

I also started to notice that people around me were slowly drinking less alcohol, too.

Maybe it was because they had no-one to drink with, or maybe they just needed to see someone do it to realise that no-one really cares if you're drinking or not.

The freedom in caring less

My experiment began rubbing off on other aspects of my life.

Emboldened by my new ability to say no, I seemed to care less about what people thought of me and stopped doing things unless I wanted to.

And the more that has happened, the stronger I feel. It's a sense of freedom that comes with pushing social boundaries without ramifications.

Don't get me wrong, I do occasionally miss a red wine and I have an inkling I'll pick up a "real glass" again one day.

But there's power in knowing that it'll be on my terms and on an occasion that I deem worth ending my game for.

Here's what I've learnt

Having a drink — any non-alcoholic drink — in a wine glass tricks my brain into thinking I'm doing what everyone else is doing. It also stops people being so quick to ask if I "need anything from the bar".

Swapping wine for a soda water also has its financial benefits. (Supplied)

Volunteering up front that I'm "not drinking right now" for an innocent reason like "playing a game with my willpower" takes the wind out of people who were suspicious about whether I was pregnant or not.

It's a great time to be alcohol-free: there are heaps of "fake beers" and "fake wines" on the market now that satisfy thirst, even compared with just five years ago when I was first pregnant.

And I'm richer – literally. Soda water with a slice of lemon is often free at the pub and I have saved so much money not buying bottles of wine.

It's also refreshing to park out the front of a venue in the rain, or just drive home when I get bored or tired. I didn't foresee how nice it would feel to be able to offer friends a lift home.

There's so much less waiting around: no lining up at the bar or for the toilets.

Not drinking alcohol has been a great conversation starter and it's empowering when people say: "Good for you".

Getting to know the strength of my willpower leads me to wonder what else I'm capable of.

title: Six passengers from hantavirus cruise ship 'in good health', minister says
text:
Six passengers from a cruise ship at the centre of a deadly hantavirus outbreak are "in good health", as the Australian government remains hopeful it will finalise plans to repatriate them within 48 hours.

Federal Health Minister Mark Butler said a French national was in a critical condition in an overseas hospital following the deaths of three other MV Hondius passengers from the rodent-borne illness.

Mr Butler said five Australians and one New Zealander are "all in good health" and in "relatively good spirits" despite their ordeal.

Australian Health Minister Mark Butler speaks to the media about the outbreak. (AAP: Lukas Coch)

Those passengers arrived in the Netherlands around 6.30am WST on Tuesday and will be medically assessed while in hotel quarantine.

They will then be flown to the Royal Australian Air Force military air base in Perth later in the week, before being "transported immediately" to the nearby Bullsbrook quarantine centre.

The Bullsbrook quarantine facility will be used to house people who were on board a cruise ship afflicted with hantavirus. (ABC News: Mitch Edgar)

"These are complex arrangements given the distance between the Netherlands and Australia," Mr Butler said on Tuesday morning.

He said authorities also needed to find a charter company and crew willing to operate the flight under strict quarantine and isolation requirements.

"We are confident they will be finalised within the 48 hour time frame the Netherlands government have given us."

Loading...

'Highly experienced'

The six passengers will isolate at the Bullsbrook facility for at least three weeks where they will be cared for by staff brought in from the National Critical Care and Trauma Response Centre in Darwin.

The cruise ship MV Hondius, affected by a hantavirus outbreak, at the port of Granadilla de Abona, in Tenerife. (Reuters: Hannah McKay)

Mr Butler said the staff were "highly experienced in dealing with infectious disease emergencies and a range of other medical emergencies both here in Australia and across the region".

The minister said further quarantine measures were possible beyond that three-week period to adhere to the World Health Organisation's recommended 42-day isolation time frame for the hantavirus.

He said out of the two dozen countries repatriating passengers from the cruise ship, Australia had put in place the strongest quarantine measures in the world.

"Those quarantine arrangements, I am very confident, will ensure that Australians can all have confidence that there is no risk to the broader Australian community from this tragedy that's happened on a cruise ship on the other side of the world."

What is hantavirus? Hantavirus is a rare rodent-borne disease usually spread through contact with rodent urine, droppings or saliva.

Symptoms can initially resemble the flu before progressing to serious respiratory or kidney complications, with some forms of the disease carrying high fatality rates.

Health authorities say the global outbreak risk is low, but investigations are continuing into how the virus spread on the Dutch expedition cruise ship MV Hondius.

Plans to deal with 'unlikely' infection

WA's Health Minister Meredith Hammat said the government was working through the detail of what would happen in the "unlikely event" one of the repatriated passengers falls ill while quarantining in Perth.

She said Perth's tertiary hospitals were equipped to deal with a potential infection.

WA Health Minister Meredith Hammat said facilities within Perth's tertiary hospitals are equipped to deal with respiratory diseases. (ABC News: Keane Bourke )

WA Premier Roger Cook said the government had been advised "the risk is next to zero" for people living near the Bullsbrook facility, and in the wider community.

"The hantavirus is not highly contagious but it is important that we take a precautionary approach," Mr Cook said.

"From that perspective we're not alarmed and people should not be concerned in relation to this particular Commonwealth government operation."

Loading...

Australia has four quarantine centres — known officially as 'Centres for National Resilience' — three of which were built during the COVID-19 pandemic at a cost of $1.37 billion.

This will be the first time the Bullsbrook facility has been used for quarantining returned travellers since it opened.

WA's Opposition Leader Basil Zempilas was pleased Bullsbrook would finally be used for its intended purpose, but was quick to point out the government recently flagged the possibility of turning the facility into a prison to manage overcrowding.

He said there were "a lot of questions" about the care of passengers if they became ill and what precautions would be put in place at medical facilities.

title: Aussie female racing drivers star at home and abroad
text:
Australia's strong cohort of young female racing drivers has shone at home and abroad, with a former F1 Academy driver achieving consecutive podiums on her Japanese debut and, history made in the GT4 Australia.

Victorian Joanne Cictone made a successful start to racing in Japan with consecutive second-place finishes during the opening round of this year's Kyojo Cup.

Cictone, a former F1 Academy driver, qualified second on the grid and maintained that position during the 10-lap sprint and 15-lap final race at the famed Fuji Speedway.

"Strong way to open the season," Cictone said following the opening weekend of the all-female racing series.

"New season, new style of racing, and a lot to take in — but really happy with how quickly we adapted and put it together.

"Huge credit to the team for the effort and support all the way through."

Fellow Australian Paige Raddatz also made her debut in the Kyojo Cup last weekend. Raddatz qualified 15th fastest, before finishing 14th and 15th in the sprint and final race.

Meanwhile, Gold Coast teenager Summer Rintoule created history at The Bend in South Australia last weekend, combining with co-driver Jarrod Hughes to win Sunday's final GT4 Australia race.

The duo started 17th on the grid, and 13th in the Silver Cup class, but navigated the safety car-affected race to claim the outright victory in their McLaren Artura GT4.

Loading Instagram content

The victory makes Rintoule the first female driver to claim an outright victory in the GT4 Australia. Zoe Woods was the first female driver to win a race in her class at Sydney Motorsport Park in 2024, competing in the GT4 Australia Pro-Am category.

“We got a lot of seconds last year but unfortunately no firsts, so this is the first of hopefully many this year for us,” Rintoule said, as reported by publication Speedcafe.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Former F1 Academy driver Aiva Anagnostiadis endured a frustrating first outing in the Italy GT4 Series at the Misano World Circuit track in Emilia-Romagna.

The Victorian never got in her BMW GT4-spec car for the opening race, after co-driver Andrea Palazzo was involved in an early incident.

Anagnostiadis started from 12th on the grid for the second race of the weekend, and maintained that position until she pitted and stepped out of the car for Palazzo to finish the race.

Unfortunately for the duo, a spin into the gravel meant they finished 16th of the 18 entrants, four laps off the winner.

title: How Jess Pegula is rallying players in midst of French Open dispute
text:
Talk of a boycott from top-ranked Aryna Sabalenka and Jannik Sinner has grabbed attention.

Behind the scenes, it's been Jessica Pegula, the 2024 US Open runner-up, organising the top tennis players in their protest with the grand slams over the share of tournament revenues devoted to prize money.

It comes naturally to Pegula, who grew up in a household of sports managers: Her parents own the NFL's Buffalo Bills and the NHL's Buffalo Sabres.

"I think it maybe does [come naturally] just because I feel like I've taken on a bit of a leadership role with it," Pegula said.

Already this year, number five ranked Pegula was named to lead a new 13-person panel to suggest changes to the women's tennis calendar, rankings points rules and the requirements about competing in certain events.

Now she's taking on the prize money issue, too.

"I'm not afraid to go up to any type of player and go like, 'Hey, are you interested in this or not?'" Pegula said.

"Some players, they don't care, sometimes they're not worried about it. Sometimes they're like, 'Yeah, for sure, I'm 100 per cent in.' I've been reaching out to players on the men's and women's side."

Sabalenka said last week the players should consider a boycott for a bigger slice of tournament revenue, while Sinner said there's a lack of respect.

"At the end of the day the players are the ones that have the big voices," Pegula said.

"It's been nice to see Aryna and Jannik kind of step out. I know a lot of other players feel the same way. But to have the two number ones very outspoken about it, that's kind of what it takes to get them to listen."

Players' share dropped in Paris

The French Open is under threat of a boycott by players. (Getty Images: Tim Clayton)

In the NFL and NHL athletes take home about 50 per cent of the revenues but at most tennis events the number isn't even half that.

The upcoming French Open is allegedly devoting under 14.9 per cent to the players — down from 15.5 per cent in 2024, according to a players' protest statement issued last week.

Roland Garros organisers announced last month they were increasing overall prize money by about 10 per cent for an overall pot of 61.7 million euros ($100.3 million), with the total amount up 5.3 million euros from last year. But the players' said that the the underlying figures tell a very different story.

Players raise fears of French Open boycott The world's leading tennis players do not feel respected, Jannik Sinner says, as fears grow of the French Open being boycotted.

"It's crazy. It's an insane difference. Obviously they're different sports. They're run differently," Pegula said, comparing tennis to the NFL.

"But tennis has been a very old-school sport. I think it's one of the things that needs to change. Sometimes change is good. Sometimes that means fighting for things."

The same group of players sent a letter a year ago to the heads of the four grand slams seeking more prize money and a greater say in decision-making. Wimbledon increased its 2025 total pot by 7 per cent, the US Open by 20 per cent and the Australian Open this past January by 16 per cent.

Still, the players say the grand slams don't match the rate of 22 per cent at regular ATP and WTA Tour events.

"The slams have kind of just gotten away with paying not that much because we're a very individual sport," Pegula said.

"It's hard to get players to come together. We're not on a salary, [whereas] football players or basketball players can afford not to play."

Jessica Pegula is in good form on clay. (Getty Images: Clive Brunskill)

Organisers at the French Open, which starts in less than two weeks, have not responded to requests for comment.

Wimbledon is due to announce its prize money next month and Pegula said the players have not had a response from the All England Club.

"We're just going to kind of continue to talk amongst ourselves," she said.

One of the big issues affecting tennis governance is that there are seven organising bodies: The four grand slams, the ATP, the WTA and the International Tennis Federation.

"That's what makes it so hard is that our sport is super fragmented. Hard to get everybody on the same page," Pegula said.

"That's why it's been huge to actually have the top 10 men and women really come together. I'm hoping that will get the slams' attention."

Pegula advances in Rome, misses Sabres Stanley Cup run

Jessica Pegula says she has missed the Buffalo Sabres' Stanley Cup run. (Getty Images: Robert Prange)

Before she gets to Paris, Pegula has been perfecting her clay-court game at the Italian Open, where she reached the quarterfinals with a 7-6 (6), 6-2 win over Anastasia Potapova on Monday. She routed Rebeka Masarova 6-0, 6-0 in the previous round.

Pegula will next face three-time Rome champion Iga Świątek, who beat four-time grand slam champion Naomi Osaka 6-2, 6-1.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

In between her matches and prize money activist duties, Pegula has been struggling to watch the Sabres' run to the play-offs for the first time in 15 years due to the time difference between the US and Europe.

The Montreal Canadiens beat the Sabres 6-2 in Game 3 on Sunday night to take a 2-1 lead in their second-round play-off series.

"My gosh, I am so upset that I have not been able to see any of the games," Pegula said.

"I would give anything to just be at a play-off game … I love it so much. It's my favourite thing."

AP

title: Gout springs relay surprise as U20 world champs team named
text:
Sprint sensation Gout Gout will double up in the 200 metre and 4x400m relay as part of a record 75-strong Australian team at the U20 world athletics championships in Oregon in August.

Gout, 18, has elected to prioritise the world juniors over the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow with the aim of emulating the legendary Usain Bolt, who won the 200m gold back in 2004 to kickstart his peerless career.

Gout is the U20 world record holder in the 200m — the discipline where he won silver at the previous edition of the world juniors in Peru in 2024.

The meet will take place at historic Hayward Field at the University of Oregon in Eugene, the spiritual home of US track and field, from August 5, just three days after the conclusion of the Glasgow Games.

"I'm really excited to get out there at World Athletics Under 20s," Gout said.

"I know it's a great stadium and place to run fast, and I feel confident I'll be ready to step up and make Australia proud.

"I'll be competing in the 200m, but I'm also excited to join in the 4x400m on the last day."

Gout's announcement comes a week after Australia's men's team won an historic bronze in the 4x400m at the World Relays in Botswana, with Luke van Ratingen, Reece Holder, Tom Reynolds and Aidan Murphy running an Oceania record of 2:55.20.

Gout will not run the 100m despite winning the event for the second straight year at the national junior championships last month.

The large under-20s squad also includes high jumper Izobelle Louison-Roe, who will be aiming to go one better than her silver from the 2024 titles in Lima.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

She is entered in the high jump and the triple jump and will decide what event she competes in later in the year.

Race walker Isaac Beacroft will back up from the Glasgow Commonwealth Games at the U20 world titles.

"I've got pretty high expectations of myself in both races, and I don't want to leave that short amount of time in between each event as an excuse for my performances," he said.

Australia won a record 15 medals two years ago in Lima.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AAP

title: Darwin company's $50 million plan to make diesel from gas condensate
text:
A Darwin-based chemical recycling company is planning to make diesel from locally extracted gas condensate, in the hopes of establishing a domestic fuel supply.



Global Resource Recovery (GRR) currently uses a failed former biodiesel plant to recycle waste oils, glycols, and other chemicals produced by Darwin's LNG export industry.

Loading...

Amid an ongoing global fuel shortage, GRR says it is now finalising agreements with two companies to purchase gas condensate extracted from gas wells in Central Australia.



The company's chief executive, Mike Everton, says the production method could result in a cheaper and more reliable source of fuel for Top End motorists.

NT Treasury data shows Darwin had the second-highest retail diesel price of all capital cities in April 2026. (ABC News: Peter Garnish)

Gas condensate is considered a by-product of gas extraction, but tens of millions of barrels are produced in Australia every year with a total value in the billions.



Geoscience Australia defines the product as a portion of natural gas, which becomes a liquid "at surface pressure and temperature conditions."



A fuel industry veteran, Mr Everton said GRR would initially refine the hydrocarbon product offshore under a "crack spread" agreement.

"The product that goes out comes back still under our sovereign flag, as our product, and it saves us about 40 to 50 cents per litre into the marketplace," he said.

Mike Everton says the production method could result in a cheaper and more reliable source of fuel. (Supplied: Mike Everton)

According to GRR, the proposed operation would eventually meet half of Darwin's total fuel demand.



Once small-scale production is underway, it plans to spend $50 million building a "tank farm" at East Arm comprising 16 stainless steel storage vessels, to be built in stages.



In a statement, Darwin Port chief executive Peter Dummett confirmed a possible site for the tank farm had been identified inside the port's lease area at East Arm and was being considered in accordance with regulatory requirements.

He said the port was working on "a number of projects" with Global Resource Recovery, "including the possibility of diesel production from NT-extracted condensate,"

While Mr Dummet said he would work with GRR, he noted "projects of this nature are dependent upon supply chain capacity."

A potential site for 16 storage tanks has been identified at East Arm. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

Not the most efficient source

Saul Kavonic, head of energy research at MST Financial, was also uncertain about the long-term feasibility of the project.

Mr Kavonic said if it were not for the current fuel crisis, GRR's plan to refine gas condensate "would probably not be the most efficient way to go about bringing in diesel."





Saul Kavonic says the proposal does not make economic sense under normal circumstances. (ABC News: John Gunn) He said gas condensate was "not a particularly good oil to make diesel from" in comparison with crude oil and said refining it overseas meant it would still be a fuel supply exposed to maritime trade disruptions.

"Australia doesn't produce a lot of crude oil, but we do produce quite a lot of condensate, including the Northern Territory, including as a by-product of a lot of LNG production", he said.

" With diesel prices skyrocketing much higher than other prices of gas and oil, something that perhaps didn't make economic sense six months ago can make more economic sense now. "

Most of the condensate produced by Darwin's LNG exporters is sent overseas. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

Possibility of Darwin fuel refinery

But refining Northern Territory diesel offshore would only be an interim measure under GRR's plan, according to its chief executive.

Mr Everton believes its proximity to both the gas industry and potential sources of low-carbon biodiesel inputs means Darwin is "perfectly placed" to host a domestic refinery.





The first 500-tonne shipment of gas condensate will leave Darwin in July. (Supplied: Global Resource Recovery NT) He said GRR's goal was to establish a "co-processing plant" capable of producing diesel from an adjustable blend of both gas condensate and biological inputs, like plant oils and animal fats.

Mr Everton said the composition of the fuel refined in Darwin would begin as "a 30 per cent blend of vegetable or animal and 70 per cent hydrocarbons," but would be altered over time according to the supply of inputs.

"This is not something where you go straight 100 per cent biofuels — it would fail like everybody else," Mr Everton said.



Mr Everton said the first 500-tonne shipment of gas condensate would leave Darwin in July.

title: Captain of virus-hit ship praises all aboard as Australians set to return home
text:
The captain of the MV Hondius has praised the patience and kindness of his crew and the guests aboard the hantavirus-hit ship as they disembark and prepare to head to their respective home countries.

In his first address to the wider public, Captain Jan Dobrogowski said the past few weeks had been "extremely challenging to us all".

"What touched me the most, what moved me the most was your patience, your discipline and also kindness," Dobrogowksi said.

Loading...

"I've witnessed your caring, your unity and quiet strength amongst everybody on board, guests and crew alike. And I must commend my crew for the courage and the selfless resolve that they showed time and again in the most difficult moments."

Video taken by Turkish YouTuber Ruhi Çenet early in the outbreak showed Dobrogowski addressing passengers and crew, sharing relevant information and delivering a message of calm.

"As a captain of Hondius, my job is to lead my crew, take care of my guests and see the ship safely to port," he said.

"So we've all been a part of this voyage and I wish nothing more to everybody, guests and crew alike, to be able to go home safely and in good health."

COVID quarantine facility finally being used for disease control Nestled away in Perth's outer fringes, this 500-bed facility was built during the COVID-19 pandemic as a quarantine centre but sat idle for years. Now, a different deadly virus will see it used for its intended purpose.

The latest information from Oceanwide Expedition said 122 guests and crew had disembarked from MV Hondius and were in the process of repatriation.

MV Hondius will transit to Rotterdam carrying 25 crew, two medical professionals and the body of a German guest who passed away on May 2.

A chartered flight carrying passengers including four Australians, a permanent resident and one New Zealand citizen arrived in the Netherlands for medical assessment at 4:45am AEST today.

None of them were displaying symptoms of the virus.

The Australian passengers of MV Hondius landed in the Netherlands for medical assessment. (Supplied: DFAT)

After 48 hours, they will board another private flight to Western Australia where they will quarantine at the Bullsbrook Centre for National Resilience for at least three weeks.

Health Minister Mark Butler said arrangements were still ongoing to secure a flight to bring the Australians home.

"This is a difficult arrangement to make," he said.

"You've got to have crew that are willing to isolate at the end of the flight."

He said he was confident the Department of Foreign Affairs would have a crew ready in the next 48 hours and the repatriation was on track.

The cruise ship, which set sail from Argentina about a month ago, is linked to seven confirmed hantavirus cases; three passengers who were on the ship have died.

The World Health Organization says the variant of the virus on the ship can be transmitted through prolonged human contact but says the risk to the public is very low.

Reuters

title: 'Relief' as Voss resigns as Carlton coach after torrid run of results
text:
Michael Voss has resigned as coach of AFL strugglers Carlton with immediate effect.

Voss decided to move on with the Blues 1-8 and effectively already out of finals contention.

"Michael is a strong leader who has led our football club with great professionalism and a genuine commitment to Carlton" Carlton president Rob Priestley said in a letter to members on Tuesday afternoon.

"Across five seasons, he has invested himself fully in the role and handled himself with impressive character throughout.

"The fact this decision is mutual reflects his selfless mindset and the club thanks him sincerely for his contribution."

The club confirmed that current assistant coach Josh Fraser will take the reigns in an interim role, while CEO Graham Wright and general manager of football Chris Davies lead the search for a new coach.

Carlton captain Patrick Cripps told a waiting media pack at Princes Park on Tuesday that Voss told him he would be leaving ahead of his teammates, saying his relationship with the outgoing coach as "really strong".

"I couldn't speak highly enough of him, he's been a great mentor and a great coach to me."

Despite the poor run of form, Carlton great Anthony Koutoufides said that he was shocked by the news.

"It was a little bit of a shock," Koutoufides said.

"Obviously, he's been under pressure now for quite a while, Voss, but to announce it at this time of the year was a little bit strange.

"It's been a tough year for Carlton supporters, only having that one win."

However, another ex-Blues coach, Mick Malthouse, said Voss would likely be relieved at finally removing the burden of coaching the fallen giants from his shoulders.

"I really think that the first thing that came to my mind was relief," Malthouse said.

"I would say that he would be sleeping so much better tonight because the pressure has been on him for so long.

"And look, it's on every coach, make no mistake about it, but there's certain clubs that bring their own pressure and Carlton are one of them."

'What do they gain?'

Mick Malthouse led Carlton for 52 AFL games from 2013 to 2015. (AAP: Joe Castro)

Malthouse also took aim at pundits who suggested that Voss, a three-time premiership-winning player with Brisbane, had to be relieved of his duties at this stage of the season.

"I think from outside — and this is a crack at all the football pundits — they keep saying 'we love Michael, but he's got to go' … under what circumstances does he have to go?" Malthouse said.

"Carlton have appointed him for a term. What do they gain now?

The 10 moments leading Essendon and Carlton into 25 years of AFL purgatory Essendon and Carlton were once mainstays when it came to the business end of the AFL season. Over the last quarter century, both clubs have fallen on hard times. Here's why.

"If he's resigned, he's resigned. But if they've pushed him, there's nothing to gain. There's absolutely nothing to gain.

"The results are insignificant because if you win or lose, it doesn't really matter. It's not going to get you into the [top] 10, so there's nothing to gain."

Voss's last game in charge was against his former club the Brisbane Lions, with Carlton fighting hard in the second half for a respectable 11-point loss against the dual reigning premiers last Friday night, a performance Malthouse said was the Blues' best this season.

"That is as good a game as they've played," Malthouse said.

"They're not a bad side. They are a quarter short of probably winning four or five more games than what they have won.

"Nonetheless, it hasn't worked."

Michael Voss has only presided over one victory from nine games this season. (Getty Images: Robert Cianflone)

Carlton has endured a torrid time off the field this season, being handed a $75,000 fine for its handling of Elijah Hollands's on-field mental health episode against Collingwood.

Voss, whose contract was to end at the end of the season, coached the Blues in 103 games between 2022 and 2026, with a winning record of 47.5 per cent.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

But Carlton is on a run of seven consecutive AFL defeats and sits 16th on the ladder, with just one win from nine games this season and only sitting above other Melbourne giants Essendon and Richmond on percentage.

Malthouse, who coached 718 AFL/VFL games, 54 of them for Carlton between 2013-15, as well as having lengthy spells at West Coast, Collingwood and the Bulldogs, understands better than anyone the pressure on coaches at AFL level, particularly at the big Victorian clubs.

The 72-year-old ABC pundit said the pressure on the big clubs was enormous.

"I think the big clubs get smashed," Malthouse said.

"Essendon gets smashed. Collingwood would be getting smashed if they weren't winning games.

"I think the Saints, North Melbourne, Melbourne, perhaps get away with it because they're not as dominant as far as the number of supporters and the media tend to let them have a little bit of grace, whereas they're unforgiving on some of the bigger clubs."

The Blues made two consecutive finals appearances under Voss in 2023 and 2024, but missed out on the eight in 2025 with just nine wins for the season.

title: Hantavirus ship travellers headed to Perth's COVID 'white elephant'
text:
Six passengers from a cruise ship at the centre of a deadly hantavirus outbreak are being transported to the 500-bed Centre for National Resilience in Bullsbrook, approximately 40 kilometres north-east of Perth.

The purpose-built quarantine facility, largely dormant since its completion in 2022 and routinely referred to as a white elephant, will now finally be used for the purpose for which it was intended.

In scenes eerily reminiscent of the early days of the COVID pandemic, the MV Hondius cruise ship last month became the site of an outbreak of the potentially fatal rodent-borne illness.

On April 11, a Dutch passenger died on board the boat, and his wife also died days later after disembarking.

The MV Hondius is docked in Tenerife. (Reuters: Stringer)

Another passenger had to be medically evacuated to South Africa where they remain in hospital.

Then, on May 2, a German passenger died on the ship.

Cruise ship passengers to quarantine in Perth Six evacuated passengers who were onboard the hantavirus-hit cruise ship are expected to land in Perth after a repatriation flight from the Canary Islands.

Now six people who had been aboard the ship, which is docked at Tenerife in the Canary Islands, are being flown to RAAF Pearce in Perth via the Netherlands before they are moved to the nearby quarantine facility.

The flight carrying four Australians, one permanent resident and one New Zealander, arrived in the Netherlands around 6:30am WST on Tuesday.

Australia's Ambassador to the Netherlands, Dr Greg French, greeted the passengers briefly at a distance as the Australian government works on repatriation arrangements so the passengers can continue their journey to Australia within 48 hours, dependent on health advice.

The passengers will be medically assessed while in the Netherlands and are expected to arrive at RAAF Pearce later this week, before they are transferred to the Bullsbrook centre.

The long distance of the flight presents some difficulties for authorities in organising the transfer, which must follow strict Australian health advice.

Medical personnel will be aboard the flight to monitor the passengers, with federal Health Minister Mark Butler saying arrangements were still ongoing and that they needed a crew willing to isolate on arrival.

The facility's history

Since its construction was completed in 2022, the Bullsbrook centre has only been used as emergency accommodation for bushfire evacuees.

Construction began on Perth's Centre for National Resilience in Bullsbrook at the end of 2021. (Supplied: Nearmap)

In April this year, WA's Corrective Services Minister Paul Papalia announced potential plans for the centre to be turned into a prison in an attempt to alleviate overcrowding in the justice system.

But other organisations, such as Shelter WA, feel the facility could be put to greater use as a shelter for rough sleepers.

Ex-COVID quarantine centre sits empty amid housing crisis While West Australians continue to feel the pressure of the housing crisis, could a 500-bed facility that's sitting empty in Perth's north go some way to easing the shortage?

Originally intended to safely house people returning home in the early days of the pandemic, the Centre for National Resilience was one of three built around the country, with the others in Brisbane and Melbourne.

The buildings cost a combined $1.37 billion — working out to about $685,000 for each bed.

The WA government spent $13 million maintaining the facility for 12 months before its management was handed over to the federal Department of Finance.

The centres were built with customised ventilation systems to prevent cross-contamination, as well as covered verandahs and separated stairways for safe movement and food delivery.

One of the family rooms at the Bullsbrook Centre for National Resilience. (Supplied: Lisa Castellas)

They also feature materials selected for easy cleaning and disinfection, on-site health support infrastructure and centralised food, laundry and waste systems.

Each self-contained unit is fitted with air-conditioning, a bathroom, television, internet and a small kitchen with a microwave and a fridge.

RAAF Pearce is Australia's main air force base in Western Australia. (ABC News: Mitch Edgar)

No symptoms

WA Health has confirmed none of the travellers are displaying symptoms of the virus and will undergo a minimum three-week quarantine period.

In a statement, the Department of Health said all appropriate officials will wear personal protective equipment and adhere to strict procedures to limit potential exposure.

The Bullsbrook quarantine facility has a capacity of 500 beds. (ABC News: Mitch Edgar)

WA's chief health officer, Clare Huppatz, reassured the community that this "rare illness presents no risk to them, given the quarantine provisions".

"This is a very rare disease, with human-to-human transmission rarer still," she said in a statement.

According to WA Health, hantavirus is spread by inhalation of contaminated rodent droppings and is not easily transmitted between people.

'Not something like COVID'

The federal health minister said that due to the lengthy potential incubation period for the hantavirus, up to 42 days, the passengers may be told to quarantine beyond three weeks.

"Obviously, we will be monitoring advice about what should happen beyond those three weeks," Mr Butler said.

This is the first time the quarantine facility will be used for disease control. (ABC News: Mitch Edgar)

Australian Medical Association WA president Kyle Hoath said the risk of the hantavirus spreading across WA was close to zero.

"It's not as readily spread as something like COVID," Dr Hoath said.

Dr Hoath assured the Bullsbrook community they were at "no greater risk than anywhere else".

"Effectively, you would have to be right in front of the person to be at risk," he said.

Dr Kyle Hoath says the virus does not transmit as readily as COVID. (ABC News: Blake Kagi)

WA Health Minister Meredith Hammat provided a statement reassuring Western Australians: "Health and safety is paramount as we activate our resources and support this national response."

"The Centre for National Resilience in Bullsbrook is a safe and appropriate facility for these returning travellers to quarantine," she said.
